svg.text: Defense in Depth — 7 Layers
Untrusted code must pass through every layer before it can do anything; each layer is independently sufficient to stop common escape attempts.
1. Application-Level Validation
Path traversal, shell-injection, compiler-flag, and size checks (internal/validate)
Closes 7 application-level holes before any process is spawned
2. Per-Job Workspace Isolation
os.MkdirTemp(0700), unique dirs, deferred cleanup, orphan reaper
Prevents workspace collisions and stale-directory buildup
3. Linux Namespaces (nsjail)
PID, mount, network, UTS, IPC, cgroup namespaces
Process sees its own PID tree, root filesystem, and no network
4. Filesystem Confinement (chroot)
chroot to per-job workspace; no /etc, no host paths visible
Read /etc/passwd, write /etc/hostname → FileNotFoundError
5. Resource Limits (rlimits + cgroup v2)
CPU time, AS, FSIZE, NPROC, STACK + memory.max, pids.max
Stops fork bombs, memory hogs, huge file writes, deep recursion
6. Capability Dropping
All Linux capabilities dropped before exec (no CAP_SYS_CHROOT, etc.)
os.chroot('/') → PermissionError (EPERM)
7. Seccomp-BPF Deny-List (Kafel)
DEFAULT ALLOW + KILL_PROCESS on ~17 dangerous syscall groups
ptrace, mount, bpf, kexec_load, init_module, chroot/pivot_root...
Verified by 15 adversarial probes (sandbox-tests.md) + unit, integration, gosec, and govulncheck in CI
